ops.insert: Seeded entries are deterministic — no API cost, stable across runs
Revised entries are rewritten by AI when the brief changes the confusion risk
Pending write-back entries must be reviewed before applying — never automatic
Applies To Future Games When: use this field to make entries portable across concepts
Counting up instead of recalling addition facts

attributes.@ml03: Player counts individual units one-by-one rather than retrieving the sum from memory
attributes.@ml04: Slow order completion time; player appears to be moving lips or tapping
attributes.@ml05: Remove counting affordances; require fast recall by imposing tight time pressure proportional to difficulty
attributes.@ml07: Any exact-sum composition game at K–2 level

ops.insert: Ignoring the carry / regrouping in two-digit sums

attributes.@ml03: Player adds digits in each column independently, forgets to regroup the tens
attributes.@ml04: Orders of 13, 15, 17 consistently under-filled by 10 (player answers 3, 5, 7)
attributes.@ml05: Design distinct visual feedback when the sum crosses a decade boundary; animate the carry visually
attributes.@ml07: Any game involving two-digit addition before fluency is established

ops.insert: Matching by visual shape rather than quantity

attributes.@ml03: Player matches order cards by visual similarity of icons rather than reading the number
attributes.@ml04: High accuracy on simple icon orders, sudden drop when icons change style or color
attributes.@ml05: Vary icon appearances across rounds; ensure the number is the only invariant cue
attributes.@ml07: Any game using icon-based representation of quantity

ops.insert: Repeated addition instead of multiplicative thinking

attributes.@ml03: Player computes 4×6 by adding 6+6+6+6 rather than recalling the product
attributes.@ml04: Long response latency on larger table entries; no error, just slow
attributes.@ml05: Make repeated-addition path non-viable by raising time pressure at difficulty 3+
attributes.@ml07: Any dispatch/allocation game using multiplicative facts

ops.insert: Treating division as repeated subtraction, not inverse multiplication

attributes.@ml03: Player computes 24÷6 by subtracting 6 repeatedly rather than recalling 6×4=24
attributes.@ml04: Correct answers on division facts but latency grows with quotient size
attributes.@ml05: Frame division problems as 'how many trucks per zone' — emphasize multiplicative language in UI copy
attributes.@ml07: Any game teaching division as partitioning or grouping

attributes.@ml03: Player places marker at degree value numerically equal to radian value (e.g., places π/3 at 60° position as if it were 3.14/3 degrees)
attributes.@ml04: Systematic placement error at non-quadrant angles; correct at 0, π/2, π, 3π/2
attributes.@ml05: Show dual-label ring (degrees and radians) that animate together; reward transitions between both forms
attributes.@ml07: Any game requiring angle representation at high school level

attributes.@ml03: Player consistently assigns sin value to x-coordinate and cos value to y-coordinate
attributes.@ml04: Mirror-image pattern of errors — all answers are correct but transposed
attributes.@ml05: Animate a point dropping to the x-axis for cos, and to the y-axis for sin, on every placement
attributes.@ml07: Any representational translation game with two-coordinate output

ops.insert: Treating the equals sign as a 'do something' command rather than a balance relationship

attributes.@ml03: Player expects the answer on the right side only; confused when variable is on the left or in the middle of the equation
attributes.@ml04: Errors spike on expressions like '? + 3 = 9' vs '9 = ? + 3' despite being identical
attributes.@ml05: Present all equation forms interchangeably from the start; use visual balance scale metaphor to reinforce symmetry
attributes.@ml07: Any algebra or missing-value game for grades 4–8

ops.insert: Inverse operation confusion — adding instead of subtracting to isolate

attributes.@ml03: To solve x+5=12, player adds 5 to both sides instead of subtracting, making x+10=12
attributes.@ml04: Consistent off-by-double-the-constant errors when solving linear equations
attributes.@ml05: Show the operation as removing/adding a load physically from the grid node; make inverse operations a tactile visual action
attributes.@ml07: Any equation-solving game where inverse operations are the core mechanic

ops.insert: Integer division — ignoring the remainder (truncation instead of ceiling)

attributes.@ml03: Player reports 4 buses for 25 students with 6-seat buses (25÷6=4.16), forgetting the remaining student who also needs a seat
attributes.@ml04: Player sends fleet with one student left behind; confusion when the game flags incomplete dispatch
attributes.@ml05: Show stragglers visually waiting at the curb after bus animation; the consequence (student left behind) must be visceral and immediate
attributes.@ml07: Any capacity or packing game where the ceiling function applies

ops.insert: Confusing per-vehicle capacity with total capacity

attributes.@ml03: Player sees 3 buses × 6 seats = 18 total, then allocates all 25 students to 3 buses without noticing overload
attributes.@ml04: Overcrowded buses dispatched confidently; player expresses surprise at the overload warning
attributes.@ml05: Animate seat counters per bus in real time as students are allocated; bus tilts or shows strain at capacity
attributes.@ml07: Any allocation game where individual unit capacity and aggregate capacity must be distinguished

ops.insert: Confusing percent with an absolute number (treating 30% as 30, not 0.3)

attributes.@ml03: Player treats a percent value as a whole number — e.g., enters 300 for a 30% problem, or subtracts 20 from a price instead of multiplying by 0.8 for a 20% discount
attributes.@ml04: Consistent off-by-factor-of-100 errors on T3/T4 prompts; correct on integer-only T9 prompts
attributes.@ml05: Auto-hint shows explicit conversion: '30% = 0.30 = 30/100'. Scaffold panel shows percent-decimal strip. For repeated errors, force prompt to specify 'type your answer as a decimal'
attributes.@ml07: Any game involving percents in applied contexts — discounts, growth, probability, tax

ops.insert: Inverse operation error — only changing one side of an equation

attributes.@ml03: For x − 27 = 14, player computes 27 − 14 = 13 (rearranges numbers on one side only). Or adds instead of subtracts when isolating the variable
attributes.@ml04: Wrong answers match 'only-one-side' results; quick submission without scaffold or hint use suggests rote but incorrect procedure
attributes.@ml05: Auto-hint: 'Whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other side.' Scaffold panel shows balance-scale metaphor with step-by-step worked example
attributes.@ml07: Any algebraic solving mechanic — missing value, balance equations, inverse operations

ops.insert: Adding fraction numerators and denominators independently (1/4 + 1/2 = 2/6)

attributes.@ml03: Player treats numerator and denominator as separate whole numbers without finding common denominators. Also: misreads '1/3 of 24' as 1 divided by (3 × 24) or similar
attributes.@ml04: Wrong answers exactly equal to numerator-and-denominator-wise operations (2/6, 3/4, etc.) across multiple similar T6 prompts
attributes.@ml05: Scaffold shows fraction bars with denominator alignment. Auto-hint: 'To add fractions, make the bottoms (denominators) match first.' For fraction-of-quantity: emphasize '1/3 of 24 = 24 ÷ 3'
attributes.@ml07: Any game using fraction operations or proportional reasoning

ops.insert: Inverting rate — using t = d × r instead of t = d ÷ r

attributes.@ml03: For 60 miles in 2 hours, player answers 120 mph (multiplies instead of divides). In rate comparison, consistently picks larger total over better per-unit rate
attributes.@ml04: Systematic inversion errors on T7/T8 prompts; may pass T9 (integer ops) cleanly, suggesting the formula is the confusion not arithmetic
attributes.@ml05: Scaffold shows rate triangle d=r×t with colour-coded compartments and worked example. Auto-hint: 'Ask: how far in 1 hour? Then multiply if you need more hours'
attributes.@ml07: Any game involving unit rates, speed, or proportional comparison (cooking, travel, resource games)

ops.insert: Sign errors in integer operations — 'minus minus is always negative'

attributes.@ml03: Player treats −3 + 5 as −8 (adds absolute values then assigns negative), or rounds 0.5 up regardless of context on T10 prompts
attributes.@ml04: Integer sum errors where answer ignores sign (−8 instead of +2). Rounding errors where player rounds to wrong place value
attributes.@ml05: Number line −15 to +15 in scaffold. Auto-hint references sign rule or 'look one digit to the right' for rounding. Worked examples using motion left/right metaphor
attributes.@ml07: Any game with integer operations or rounding under time pressure

ops.insert: Swapping complementary and supplementary angle definitions

attributes.@ml03: Player treats complementary as 'adds to 180°' and supplementary as 'adds to 90°' — exact swap of the two definitions
attributes.@ml04: Consistent wrong answers that are exactly the opposite type (player solves a supplementary problem as complementary and vice versa)
attributes.@ml05: Scaffold shows labelled right-angle (90°) and straight-angle (180°) arc diagrams. Auto-hint: 'Complementary → 90° (corner of a square). Supplementary → 180° (straight line)'
attributes.@ml07: Any game using angle relationships or geometric diagrams with complementary/supplementary pairs

ops.insert: Expected value confusion — averaging outcomes without weighting by probability

attributes.@ml03: Player computes expected value as simple average of outcomes (ignoring unequal probabilities) or sums probabilities themselves rather than probability × value
attributes.@ml04: High error rate on T12 while performing well on T9 (integer ops); answers match simple-average computation
attributes.@ml05: Scaffold shows EV table: Outcome | Probability | Product | Total. Auto-hint: 'Expected value = sum of (value × probability). Example: dice showing 6 with p=1/6 contributes 6 × 1/6 = 1'
attributes.@ml07: Any game with probability and EV — loot drops, risk decisions, choices with uncertain outcomes

attributes.@ml03: Player compares only the first (leftmost) digit of each expression rather than computing the full total. Example: ranks 9−1 above 4+7 because 9 > 4, ignoring that 9−1=8 and 4+7=11. Works whenever the largest starting number also produces the largest result; fails silently when subtraction or different addends invert the relationship.
attributes.@ml04: High accuracy on expressions where the largest first digit corresponds to the largest total (e.g., 8+1 vs 3+2 — both heuristic and correct answer align). Accuracy drops sharply on contrast-set pairs where a lower first digit wins (e.g., 4+8 vs 9−3): player consistently mis-ranks these. Error pattern disappears when all-addition expressions are used and addends are near-equal, reappears when subtraction or large addend differences are introduced.
attributes.@ml05: CONTRAST-SET RULE: Every difficulty tier must include at least one pair where the smaller first digit produces the larger total (e.g., 4+9 vs 9−2; 3+8 vs 8−4). These pairs must appear in the first 3 rounds at each new difficulty level to surface the heuristic before it is reinforced. FEEDBACK RULE: On mis-ordering a contrast pair, amber glow highlights the pair; the kid-voice line says 'The first number is big, but what about what's left after taking away?' — no total revealed. LEVEL-DESIGN RULE: Do not allow 4+ consecutive rounds where leftmost-biggest heuristic is correct; this will cement the bypass. MITIGATION: Include at least one round per session where a near-miss contrast set appears (e.g., 5+4 vs 9−1; difference = 0 — a tie) to force comparison rather than estimation.
attributes.@ml07: Any ordering game using arithmetic expressions where the leading digit of the expression is not a reliable proxy for the result's magnitude — especially when mixed +/− or subtraction with large minuends is used.

attributes.@ml03: Player treats the operation sign (+/−) as irrelevant or decorative — orders expressions using only the number pair, ignoring whether the sign is addition or subtraction. Example: ranks 6+2 and 6−2 identically (as 'the 6-and-2 kid'), or consistently orders by largest number in the expression regardless of sign. Related manifestation: player applies a consistent single rule ('always add') even when half the expressions use subtraction.
attributes.@ml04: Accurate ordering on same-sign rounds (all-addition or all-subtraction). Accuracy collapses on mixed-sign rounds — especially when pairs share the same digit set but differ only in sign (e.g., 7+2 vs 7−2). Error pattern: wrong-order answers match what the correct order would be if all expressions were additions. Player may show no hesitation or re-check behaviour on mixed-sign rounds, suggesting the sign is not being read at all.
attributes.@ml05: CONTRAST-SET RULE: For every difficulty tier that introduces subtraction, the first new-concept round must include a same-digit, opposite-sign pair (e.g., 5+3 and 5−3 in the same lineup). This is the minimum viable probe for sign blindness. FEEDBACK RULE: On mis-ordering a same-digit, opposite-sign pair, amber glow + kid-voice line emphasises the sign: 'This tray says PLUS 3 — that's different from MINUS 3!' The sign character (+ or −) on the wrong kid's tray pulses visually for 2 seconds after the amber glow. VISUAL DESIGN RULE: The operation sign must be at least 1.5× the font weight and colour-differentiated from the numeral (e.g., + in green, − in red) from the earliest level at which subtraction appears. LEVEL-DESIGN RULE: Never introduce subtraction for the first time on a round that also introduces a longer line or a new constraint — sign blindness is most likely to be masked when other cognitive load increases simultaneously.
attributes.@ml07: Any arithmetic expression game where multiple operation types coexist in the same round — especially any K–3 ordering, sorting, or comparison game that transitions from single-operation to mixed-operation content.

ops.insert: M6 — Zero-subtraction confusion (zero changes nothing, overgeneralised)

attributes.@ml03: Player has correctly learned that adding zero leaves a number unchanged (n+0=n) but over-generalises this identity to subtraction: assumes n−0 is somehow different from n, or misremembers it as 0. Also appears as: treating 9−0 as equivalent to 0 ('you took away nothing so there's nothing left'), or treating 9−0 and 0+9 as producing different values. The root is a memorised 'zero rule' applied without understanding of why it holds.
attributes.@ml04: Mis-ranking of expressions involving −0 compared to expressions with similar but non-zero subtrahends (e.g., ranks 8+1 above 9−0 despite 9−0=9 > 8+1=9 being a tie, or ranks 7+2 above 9−0 despite 7+2 < 9−0). Player may hesitate or verbalise uncertainty specifically on −0 expressions ('wait, minus zero...') while handling all other expressions confidently. Correct-on-addition-zero (n+0) combined with wrong-on-subtraction-zero (n−0) is the diagnostic signature.
attributes.@ml05: CONTRAST-SET RULE: Pair n−0 with n+0 and with (n−1) in the same lineup at least once per session once subtraction is introduced. Example round: 9−0, 9+0, 9−1 in a three-kid line. This is the minimum probe for zero overgeneralisation. FEEDBACK RULE: On any mis-ordering involving a −0 expression, amber glow + kid-voice line: 'I took away zero snacks — so I still have all of them!' Do not reveal the total numerically; the line 'I still have ALL of them' is the cue. CONCEPTUAL BRIDGE: In the tutorial/intro for levels containing subtraction, show a brief animation of a kid arriving with a tray showing '8−0' and visually removing zero items — the tray stays full. Label this 'taking nothing away.' LEVEL-DESIGN RULE: Do not combine a −0 expression with a same-minuend +0 expression on the same kid until the player has seen at least two prior rounds with −0 alone — otherwise it becomes a pattern-match rather than a concept.
attributes.@ml07: Any K–2 arithmetic game that introduces both addition-of-zero and subtraction-of-zero as expression types — especially ordering, comparison, or balance games where zero-operand expressions must be evaluated relative to non-zero ones.

ops.insert: M7 — Bigger number first = bigger result

attributes.@ml03: Player assumes that an expression with a large leading number must produce a large result, regardless of the operation or the second operand. Distinct from M4 (leftmost digit dominance) in that M7 specifically governs the interaction between a large minuend and a large subtrahend: the player correctly notes the first number is big but fails to account for how much is being taken away. Examples: ranks 9−6 above 4+4 (9−6=3 < 4+4=8); ranks 9−7 above 2+6 (9−7=2 < 2+6=8). Mechanistically: the player anchors on the minuend and applies only partial processing of the subtrahend.
attributes.@ml04: Correct ordering when the large-first-number expression uses a small subtrahend (9−1, 8−2 — heuristic and correct answer agree). Mis-ordering specifically when the large-first-number expression has a large subtrahend that brings the total below a competing expression with a small first number but large addend. Diagnostic signature: player's error rate on 'big-minus-big vs small-plus-big' pairs is significantly higher than their error rate on any other expression type in the same session. Player may correctly compute 9−6=3 when prompted directly but still rank 9−6 above 4+4 in the ordering context — suggesting the ranking decision is made before or instead of the computation.
attributes.@ml05: CONTRAST-SET RULE: Include at least one 'big-minus-big vs small-plus-big' pair at every difficulty tier containing subtraction. The canonical form is: (L−H) vs (S+M) where L−H < S+M, e.g., 9−6 vs 2+5, 8−5 vs 1+6, 10−8 vs 3+4. FEEDBACK RULE: On mis-ordering this pair type, amber glow + kid-voice line from the smaller-looking but actually-larger kid: 'My tray says plus — so I end up with more!' Do not reveal totals. LEVEL-DESIGN RULE: Introduce 'big-minus-big' expressions (subtrahend ≥ 4) only after the player has demonstrated correct ordering of 'big-minus-small' expressions (subtrahend ≤ 2) in at least 2 prior rounds — this prevents M7 being triggered before the player has any handle on subtraction magnitude. SEQUENCE RULE: When a 'big-minus-big' expression first appears, pair it with an addition expression whose total is clearly and visibly larger (difference ≥ 3) before introducing near-miss pairs (difference = 1 or 2). This establishes the category before testing its edge. BROADER PRINCIPLE: M7 and M4 often co-occur; a player showing both will systematically mis-rank any expression where the leading digit misleads. Design mixed-sign rounds to probe for co-occurrence by including at least one M4-probe pair and one M7-probe pair per mixed-sign session.
attributes.@ml07: Any game where arithmetic expressions with subtraction and large minuends must be evaluated or compared against addition expressions — particularly ordering, ranking, or magnitude-comparison games at K–3 level. Also relevant to any game where intuitive magnitude estimation is a viable bypass route for arithmetic computation.
